```{r}
m0c  <- 1.00
m1c  <- 1.45   # M^d larger → P1 ≈ 0.69

P0c  <- M_s / m0c;  P1c <- M_s / m1c
Pmax_c <- 1.30;  Mmax_c <- 1.80
P_seq_c <- seq(0, Pmax_c, length.out = 300)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(M = P_seq_c * m0c, P = P_seq_c),
            aes(M, P), color = "black",    linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(M = P_seq_c * m1c, P = P_seq_c),
            aes(M, P), color = "firebrick", linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("segment", x = M_s, xend = M_s, y = 0, yend = Pmax_c * 1.02,
           color = "black", linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = M_s, y = P0c, yend = P0c,
           linetype = "dotted", color = "black",    linewidth = 0.6) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = M_s, y = P1c, yend = P1c,
           linetype = "dotted", color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(aes(x = M_s, y = P0c), color = "black",    size = 2.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x = M_s, y = P1c), color = "firebrick", size = 2.5) +
  annotate("text", x = -0.005, y = P0c,
           label = expression(P["0,t"]), hjust = 1, size = 4, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = -0.005, y = P1c,
           label = expression(P["1,t"]), hjust = 1, size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
  annotate("text", x = M_s + 0.04, y = Pmax_c * 0.96,
           label = expression(M[t]^s), hjust = 0, size = 4, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = M_s, y = -0.05,
           label = expression(bar(M)[t]), size = 4, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = Pmax_c * m0c * 0.4, y = Pmax_c * 0.65,
           label = expression(P[t]*M^d*(i["0,t"]*","~Y["0,t"])),
           hjust = 0, size = 4, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = Pmax_c * m1c * 0.63, y = Pmax_c * 0.80,
           label = expression(P[t]*M^d*(i["1,t"]*","~Y["1,t"])),
           hjust = 0, size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) + scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, Mmax_c), ylim = c(0, Pmax_c), clip = "off") +
  labs(x = expression(M[t]), y = expression(P[t])) + th_mm
```

---

### Summary of endogenous variable responses

| Variable | (a) $G_{t+1} \uparrow$ | (b) $A_{t+1} \uparrow$ | (c) $A_t$ and $A_{t+1} \uparrow$ |
|----------|:----------------------:|:----------------------:|:---------------------------------:|
| $N_t^*$  | Unchanged | Unchanged | $\uparrow$ |
| $w_t^*$  | Unchanged | Unchanged | $\uparrow$ |
| $Y_t^*$  | Unchanged | Unchanged | $\uparrow$ |
| $r_t^*$  | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Ambiguous |
| $i_t^*$  | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Ambiguous |
| $P_t^*$  | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Ambiguous ($\downarrow$ if $Y$ effect dominates) |

**Key takeaways.** (1) Pure demand shocks ($G_{t+1}$ and $A_{t+1}$) leave the real allocation unchanged — employment, the real wage, and output are all supply-determined. Only $r_t^*$ and $P_t^*$ move, and they move in opposite directions for the two shocks. (2) A permanent productivity increase is the only shock here that raises $Y_t^*$, $N_t^*$, and $w_t^*$, because it works through the supply side. (3) The response of $r_t^*$ (and hence $P_t^*$) to the permanent shock is ambiguous: the supply-side expansion (rightward $Y^s$) tends to lower $r_t$, while the demand-side expansion (rightward IS) tends to raise it.

---

# Exercise 19.6 — MPC and the Slope of the IS Curve

**Problem.** Consider two different versions of the basic neoclassical model. In one, the
marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is relatively large; in the other the MPC is
relatively small.

**(a)** Show how a higher or lower value of the MPC affects the slope of the IS curve.

**(b)** Suppose that there is an increase in $A_t$. Show graphically how this impacts
equilibrium $r_t$ in the two cases — one in which the MPC is relatively large, and one in
which the MPC is relatively small.

---

## Solution

### Setup: the IS curve and the spending multiplier

The IS curve summarizes combinations $(Y_t, r_t)$ consistent with goods-market
equilibrium and optimal household and firm behavior:

$$Y^*_t = C^d(Y^*_t - G_t,\, Y_{t+1} - G_{t+1},\, r_t) + I^d(r_t,\, A_{t+1},\, K_t) + G_t.$$
Re-writing in a more compact form $Y^*_t=Y^d_t(Y^*_t,r_t)$ and total differentiating:

$$d Y^*_t=\frac{\partial Y^d_t}{\partial Y_t} dY^*_t+\frac{\partial Y^d_t}{\partial r_t} dr_t.$$
Notice that $\frac{\partial Y^d_t}{\partial Y_t}=\frac{\partial C^d_t}{\partial Y_t}=MPC,$ so we can write

$$dY^*_t=\text{MPC} dY^*_t+\frac{\partial Y^d_t}{\partial r_t} dr_t,$$
which simplifies to:

$$\frac{dY^*_t}{dr_t} = \frac{1}{1-\text{MPC}} \cdot \frac{\partial Y^d_t}{\partial r_t} < 0,$$
Because both consumption and investment are decreasing in $r_t.$

The factor $1/(1-\text{MPC})$ is the **spending multiplier**. When MPC is large, the multiplier is large: any fall in autonomous expenditure (caused by a higher $r_t$) is amplified through many rounds of induced consumption cuts, generating a large drop in equilibrium output. The IS curve is therefore **flatter** (more elastic in $Y_t$) when MPC is large, and **steeper** when MPC is small.

---

### Part (a) — IS curve slope and MPC

The figure below derives the IS curve via the Keynesian cross for both a high-MPC and a low-MPC economy. Defining the *autonomous expenditure* 

$$E_0:=C^d(- G_t,\, Y_{t+1} - G_{t+1},\, r_t) + I^d(r_t,\, A_{t+1},\, K_t) + G_t.$$ 

as the aggregate expenditure when $Y_t=0$, the **same** increase in $r_t$ shifts the expenditure schedule down by the same amount $\Delta E_0 < 0$ in both panels (since $E_0$ does not depend on MPC). The spending multiplier then amplifies this shift differently: $|\Delta Y^*| = |\Delta E_0|/(1-\text{MPC})$ is larger when MPC is larger.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ex196-part-a
#| fig-cap: "Keynesian cross (top row) and IS curve (bottom row) for a **high-MPC** economy (left column) and a **low-MPC** economy (right column). Both top panels show the **same** vertical shift $\\Delta E_0$ (identical for both economies since $E_0$ does not depend on MPC), but the high-MPC expenditure schedule is steeper, so the same $\\Delta E_0$ produces a **larger** $\\Delta Y^*$ (left) than with low MPC (right). Plotting both IS curves on the **same axis** makes the slope difference visible: the high-MPC IS is **flat** while the low-MPC IS is **steep**."
#| fig-alt: "Four panels in a 2x2 grid with shared axes. Top row: Keynesian-cross diagrams with the same vertical gap between expenditure schedules (ΔE0) but different ΔY — large in the high-MPC panel, small in the low-MPC panel. Bottom row: IS curves on the same (Y,r) scale — flat IS on the left, steep IS on the right."
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 9

library(tidyverse)

# ── Fixed parameters (identical for both economies) ──────────────────────────
r_lo    <- 0.02;  r_hi    <- 0.08
e0_base <- 60;    e0_sens <- 200
mpc_hi  <- 0.75;  mpc_lo  <- 0.30

E0_lo <- e0_base - e0_sens * r_lo   # = 56  (at r_lo)
E0_hi <- e0_base - e0_sens * r_hi   # = 44  (at r_hi)
# NOTE: E0_lo - E0_hi = e0_sens*(r_hi - r_lo) = 12 — same for both economies.

# Equilibria: Y* = E0 / (1 - MPC)
Y_lo_hi <- E0_lo / (1 - mpc_hi)    # = 224   high-MPC, low-r
Y_lo_lo <- E0_lo / (1 - mpc_lo)    # ≈  80   low-MPC,  low-r

# ── Shared axis ranges (critical: same scale lets slope differences show) ─────
Ymax_kc <- Y_lo_hi * 1.25          # = 280  (both KC panels)
Ymax_is <- Y_lo_hi * 1.20          # ≈ 269  (both IS panels)
rmax_is <- 0.14

# ── Shared theme ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
th <- theme_minimal(base_size = 11, base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#fdfcf8", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#fdfcf8", color = NA),
    panel.grid       = element_blank(),
    axis.line        = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.4),
    axis.ticks       = element_blank(),
    axis.text        = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x     = element_text(hjust = 1, vjust = 0,  size = 12),
    axis.title.y     = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0, vjust = 1, size = 12),
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold",
                                    color = "#1a3a6b", hjust = 0.5)
  )

# ── Helper: KC + IS panels for a given MPC ────────────────────────────────────
make_kc_is <- function(mpc, label_str) {

  Y_lo <- E0_lo / (1 - mpc)
  Y_hi <- E0_hi / (1 - mpc)

  Y_seq <- seq(0, Ymax_kc, length.out = 300)
  Yd_lo <- E0_lo + mpc * Y_seq
  Yd_hi <- E0_hi + mpc * Y_seq

  # ── Keynesian-cross panel (shared Ymax_kc) ───────────────────────────────
  p_kc <- ggplot() +
    # 45-degree line
    geom_line(aes(x = Y_seq, y = Y_seq), color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
    # Expenditure schedules
    geom_line(aes(x = Y_seq, y = Yd_lo), color = "#2255cc",  linewidth = 1.0) +
    geom_line(aes(x = Y_seq, y = Yd_hi), color = "firebrick", linewidth = 1.0) +
    # Vertical drop lines to equilibria
    geom_segment(aes(x = Y_lo, xend = Y_lo, y = 0, yend = Y_lo),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "#2255cc",  linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = Y_hi, xend = Y_hi, y = 0, yend = Y_hi),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
    annotate("text",
             x =-20, y = (E0_hi + E0_lo) / 2,
             label = expression(Delta * E[0]), size = 4, color = "grey30", hjust = 0) +
    # ΔY brace above x-axis
    annotate("segment", x = Y_hi, xend = Y_lo,
             y = 20, yend = 20,
             arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.07, "in"), type = "open"),
             color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.6) +
    annotate("text",
             x = (Y_hi + Y_lo) / 2, y = 35,
             label = expression(Delta * Y^"*"), size = 4, color = "grey30") +
    # Curve labels
    annotate("text",
             x = Ymax_kc * 0.1, y = 20+(E0_lo + mpc *Ymax_kc*0.1) ,
             label = expression(Y[t]^d ~ (r[lo])),
             color = "#2255cc", size = 4) +
    annotate("text",
             x = Ymax_kc * 0.9, y = -20+(E0_hi + mpc *Ymax_kc*0.9),
             label = expression(Y[t]^d ~ (r[hi])),
             color = "firebrick", size = 4) +
    # x-axis labels
    annotate("text", x = Y_lo, y = -10,
             label = expression(Y[lo]^"*"), size = 4, color = "#2255cc") +
    annotate("text", x = Y_hi, y = -10,
             label = expression(Y[hi]^"*"), size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, Ymax_kc), ylim = c(0, Ymax_kc),
                    clip = "off") +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    labs(x = expression(Y[t]), y = expression(Y[t]^d), title = label_str) +
    th

  # ── IS-curve panel (shared Ymax_is — this is what reveals the slope diff) ──
  r_seq <- seq(0, rmax_is, length.out = 300)
  Y_is  <- (e0_base - e0_sens * r_seq) / (1 - mpc)
  is_df <- tibble(Y = Y_is, r = r_seq) |>
    filter(Y > 0, Y <= Ymax_is)

  # IS label: near the low-r (rightmost) end of the visible IS curve
  is_label_x <- max(is_df$Y) * 0.90
  is_label_y <- is_df$r[which.min(abs(is_df$Y - is_label_x))] + rmax_is * 0.06

  p_is <- ggplot(is_df, aes(x = Y, y = r)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 1.1) +
    # Equilibrium points
    geom_point(aes(x = Y_lo, y = r_lo), color = "#2255cc",  size = 2.5) +
    geom_point(aes(x = Y_hi, y = r_hi), color = "firebrick", size = 2.5) +
    # Drop lines — low r
    geom_segment(aes(x = Y_lo, xend = Y_lo, y = 0, yend = rmax_is),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "#2255cc",  linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = 0,    xend = Y_lo, y = r_lo, yend = r_lo),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "#2255cc",  linewidth = 0.6) +
    # Drop lines — high r
    geom_segment(aes(x = Y_hi, xend = Y_hi, y = 0, yend = rmax_is),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = 0,    xend = Y_hi, y = r_hi, yend = r_hi),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
    # ΔY brace on x-axis (visually larger in the high-MPC panel)
    annotate("segment", x = Y_hi, xend = Y_lo,
             y = 0.004, yend = 0.004,
             arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.07, "in"), type = "open"),
             color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.6) +
    annotate("text",
             x = (Y_hi + Y_lo) / 2, y = 0.01,
             label = expression(Delta * Y^"*"), size = 3.3, color = "grey30") +
    # IS label
    annotate("text", x = is_label_x+10, y = is_label_y,
             label = "IS", size = 4, fontface = "italic") +
    # Axis value labels
    annotate("text", x = -8, y = r_lo,
             label = expression(r[lo]), size = 4, color = "#2255cc") +
    annotate("text", x = -8, y = r_hi,
             label = expression(r[hi]), size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
    annotate("text", x = Y_lo, y = -10,
             label = expression(Y[lo]^"*"), size = 4, color = "#2255cc") +
    annotate("text", x = Y_hi, y = -10,
             label = expression(Y[hi]^"*"), size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, Ymax_kc), ylim = c(0, rmax_is), clip = "off") +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    labs(x = expression(Y[t]), y = expression(r[t])) +
    th

  list(kc = p_kc, is = p_is)
}

panels_hi <- make_kc_is(mpc = mpc_hi, label_str = "High MPC = 0.75")
panels_lo <- make_kc_is(mpc = mpc_lo, label_str = "Low MPC = 0.30")

# Convert to grobs and equalise column widths so KC and IS rows align vertically
g_hi_kc <- ggplotGrob(panels_hi$kc)
g_lo_kc <- ggplotGrob(panels_lo$kc)
g_hi_is <- ggplotGrob(panels_hi$is)
g_lo_is <- ggplotGrob(panels_lo$is)

maxW_hi <- grid::unit.pmax(g_hi_kc$widths, g_hi_is$widths)
g_hi_kc$widths <- maxW_hi;  g_hi_is$widths <- maxW_hi

maxW_lo <- grid::unit.pmax(g_lo_kc$widths, g_lo_is$widths)
g_lo_kc$widths <- maxW_lo;  g_lo_is$widths <- maxW_lo

gridExtra::grid.arrange(g_hi_kc, g_lo_kc, g_hi_is, g_lo_is,
  ncol = 2,
  heights = c(1.1, 1)
)
```

**Key result.** The IS curve is **flatter** when MPC is large and **steeper** when MPC is
small. The mechanism is the spending multiplier $1/(1-\text{MPC})$: a larger MPC
amplifies any interest-rate-driven reduction in autonomous expenditure into a larger
output contraction, so a small increase in $r_t$ produces a large fall in $Y_t^*$.
Conversely, with a small MPC the multiplier is close to one, autonomous and total
expenditure are nearly the same thing, and the IS curve is steep.

---

### Part (b) — Effect of an increase in $A_t$ on $r_t^*$

An increase in $A_t$ works **entirely through the supply side**:

1. It raises the marginal product of labor ($\text{MPN}_t = A_t F_N \uparrow$), shifting
   **labor demand** right in the $(N_t, w_t)$ plane.
2. Higher equilibrium employment and higher $A_t$ both raise output, shifting
   **$Y^s$ rightward** in the IS–$Y^s$ diagram.
3. The IS curve is **unaffected** — $A_t$ does not appear in the investment or
   consumption demand functions (only $A_{t+1}$ matters for investment).

Because $Y^s$ is vertical, output $Y_t^*$ is fully determined by supply; the real
interest rate $r_t^*$ then adjusts along the IS curve to clear the goods market. A
rightward shift of $Y^s$ by $\Delta Y$ moves the equilibrium down the IS curve.

**The MPC determines how far $r_t^*$ must fall:**

$$\Delta r_t^* = MPC \cdot \left(\frac{\partial Y^d_t}{\partial r_t}\right)^{-1} \cdot \Delta Y^*_t.$$

- With a **flat IS** (high MPC): moving from $Y_0^*$ to $Y_1^*$ along the IS requires
  only a **small** fall in $r_t^*$, because the IS curve is nearly horizontal.
- With a **steep IS** (low MPC): the same rightward shift in $Y^s$ requires a
  **large** fall in $r_t^*$ to move along the steep IS to the new output level.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ex196-part-b
#| fig-cap: "Effect of an increase in $A_t$ in the IS–$Y^s$ diagram. $Y_0^s = Y_1^s$ are placed at the **same** positions in both panels, so $\\Delta Y$ is identical. Because the IS slope differs across panels, the required fall in $r_t^*$ is **small** with a flat IS (high MPC, left) and **large** with a steep IS (low MPC, right)."
#| fig-alt: "Two IS-Ys diagrams on a shared scale. Both show identical rightward Ys shifts. The flat IS (left) requires only a small fall in r; the steep IS (right) requires a large fall in r."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 9

# ── Part (b): fix Y0 = 100 for both panels; derive e0_base per-MPC so that
#    the IS passes through (Y0, r0).  Same dY in both panels → different Δr. ──

e0_sens <- 200
Y0 <- 100;  r0 <- 0.05;  dY <- 10;  Y1 <- Y0 + dY

# For IS to pass through (Y0, r0): e0_base = Y0*(1-mpc) + e0_sens*r0
e0_hi <- Y0 * (1 - 0.75) + e0_sens * r0   # = 25 + 10 = 35
e0_lo <- Y0 * (1 - 0.30) + e0_sens * r0   # = 70 + 10 = 80

# New equilibrium r after supply shock: r1 = (e0_base - (1-mpc)*Y1) / e0_sens
r1_hi <- (e0_hi - (1 - 0.75) * Y1) / e0_sens   # = 7.5/200 = 0.0375  → Δr = 0.0125
r1_lo <- (e0_lo - (1 - 0.30) * Y1) / e0_sens   # = 3.0/200 = 0.015   → Δr = 0.035

# IS at r = 0: Y_max_hi = 35/0.25 = 140;  Y_max_lo = 80/0.70 ≈ 114.3
# Shared axes: Ymax = 155, rmax = 0.08
Ymax_b <- 155;  rmax_b <- 0.08

make_isys <- function(mpc, e0_b, r1, label_str) {

  r_seq <- seq(0, rmax_b, length.out = 300)
  Y_is  <- (e0_b - e0_sens * r_seq) / (1 - mpc)
  is_df <- tibble(Y = Y_is, r = r_seq) |> filter(Y > 0, Y <= Ymax_b)

  # IS label near the low-r end of the visible IS
  is_label_x <- max(is_df$Y) * 0.90
  is_label_y <- is_df$r[which.min(abs(is_df$Y - is_label_x))] + rmax_b * 0.07

  th <- theme_minimal(base_size = 11, base_family = "serif") +
    theme(
      plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#fdfcf8", color = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#fdfcf8", color = NA),
      panel.grid       = element_blank(),
      axis.line        = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.4),
      axis.ticks       = element_blank(),
      axis.text        = element_blank(),
      axis.title.x     = element_text(hjust = 1, vjust = 0,  size = 12),
      axis.title.y     = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0, vjust = 1, size = 12),
      plot.title       = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold",
                                      color = "#1a3a6b", hjust = 0.5)
    )

  ggplot() +
    # IS curve
    geom_line(data = is_df, aes(x = Y, y = r), color = "black", linewidth = 1.1) +
    # Baseline Y^s
    geom_segment(aes(x = Y0, xend = Y0, y = 0, yend = rmax_b * 0.92),
                 color = "black", linewidth = 1.0) +
    # Shifted Y^s
    geom_segment(aes(x = Y1, xend = Y1, y = 0, yend = rmax_b * 0.92),
                 color = "firebrick", linewidth = 1.0) +
    # Arrow between Y^s curves
    annotate("segment",
             x = Y0 + dY * 0.15, xend = Y1 - dY * 0.15,
             y = rmax_b * 0.80,  yend = rmax_b * 0.80,
             arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.10, "in"), type = "closed"),
             color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.7) +
    # Horizontal drop lines
    geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = Y0, y = r0, yend = r0),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "black",     linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = Y1, y = r1, yend = r1),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
    # Equilibrium points
    geom_point(aes(x = Y0, y = r0), color = "black",     size = 2.5) +
    geom_point(aes(x = Y1, y = r1), color = "firebrick", size = 2.5) +
    # Δr annotation (visually small left, large right)
    annotate("segment",
             x = Ymax_b * 0.06, xend = Ymax_b * 0.06,
             y = r1, yend = r0,
             arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.07, "in"), type = "open"),
             color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.6) +
    annotate("text",
             x = Ymax_b * 0.10, y = (r0 + r1) / 2,
             label = expression(Delta * r[t]^"*"),
             size = 4, color = "grey30", hjust = 0) +
    # IS label
    annotate("text", x = is_label_x+4, y = is_label_y,
             label = "IS", size = 4, fontface = "italic") +
    # r labels
    annotate("text", x = -1, y = r0,
             label = expression(r["0,t"]^"*"),
             hjust = 1, size = 4, color = "black") +
    annotate("text", x = -1, y = r1,
             label = expression(r["1,t"]^"*"),
             hjust = 1, size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
    # Y labels
    annotate("text", x = Y0, y = -0.003,
             label = expression(Y["0,t"]^"*"),
             hjust = 0.5, size = 4, color = "black") +
    annotate("text", x = Y1, y = -0.003,
             label = expression(Y["1,t"]^"*"),
             hjust = 0.5, size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
    # Y^s labels
    annotate("text",
             x = Y0 + 5, y = rmax_b * 0.88,
             label = expression(Y[0]^{s}), size = 4, color = "black") +
    annotate("text",
             x = Y1 + 5, y = rmax_b * 0.88,
             label = expression(Y[1]^{s}), size = 4, color = "firebrick") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, Ymax_b), ylim = c(0, rmax_b), clip = "off") +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    labs(x = expression(Y[t]), y = expression(r[t]), title = label_str) +
    th
}

p_hi <- make_isys(mpc = 0.75, e0_b = e0_hi, r1 = r1_hi, label_str = "High MPC (flat IS)")
p_lo <- make_isys(mpc = 0.30, e0_b = e0_lo, r1 = r1_lo, label_str = "Low MPC (steep IS)")

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_hi, p_lo, ncol = 2)
```

**Conclusion.** The same supply-side shock (a rightward shift in $Y^s$ driven by the
increase in $A_t$) produces **different interest-rate responses** depending on the MPC:

| | High MPC (flat IS) | Low MPC (steep IS) |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| $\Delta Y_t^*$ | Same | Same |
| $\Delta r_t^*$ | **Small** $\downarrow$ | **Large** $\downarrow$ |

The intuition is that the IS curve describes how much $r_t$ must fall to induce the
extra demand needed to absorb the additional supply. With a large MPC, a small fall in
$r_t$ triggers a large consumption response (amplified by the multiplier), so little
adjustment in $r_t$ suffices. With a small MPC, the multiplier is weak and $r_t$ must
fall substantially to stimulate enough demand.
